title: Blue Guardian Pricing Guide 2026: All Plans, Costs & Hidden Fees

meta_description: Blue Guardian starts at $97/mo with 80% profit split and two-phase evaluation. Full breakdown of all plans, fees, and hidden costs for 2026.
excerpt: Blue Guardian charges $97/mo for its two-phase evaluation with an 80% profit split. Here's every cost, fee, and plan detail for 2026.
content:
Blue Guardian Pricing Overview 2026

Blue Guardian has positioned itself as one of the more straightforward prop firms in the funded trading space. If you've been hunting for a firm with transparent rules and no hidden gotchas buried in the fine print, this guide breaks down exactly what you'll pay, what you'll get, and where the friction points are before you hand over your credit card.

The firm currently holds a 3.8/5 rating and is particularly well-suited for forex and multi-asset traders, as well as those running algorithmic strategies or Expert Advisors (EAs). That last point matters — many prop firms quietly throttle or ban automated trading, but Blue Guardian explicitly permits it.

Before diving into the numbers, here's the short version: Blue Guardian runs a standard two-phase evaluation model. You pay an upfront challenge fee, pass two sets of profit targets while respecting drawdown limits, and then trade a funded account with an 80% profit split. No subscription fees on the funded account — the challenge fee is a one-time cost.

Blue Guardian Challenge Plans and Costs

Blue Guardian offers account sizes ranging from small starter challenges up to larger capital allocations. The pricing scales with the account size you're targeting, which is standard across the prop firm industry.

The base entry point sits at $97/month for their challenge offering, making it competitive with mid-tier prop firms. Here's a breakdown of what that buys you:

  • Two-phase evaluation: Phase 1 requires hitting a profit target while staying within daily and overall drawdown limits. Phase 2 repeats the process at a slightly reduced profit target to confirm consistency.
  • 80% profit split: Once funded, you keep 80 cents of every dollar you earn. This is competitive — many firms offer 70-75% on standard plans.
  • EA and automated strategy support: Full permission to run bots, copy trading setups, or algorithmic systems during both the challenge and funded phases.
  • MetaTrader 4 and MT5 only: No cTrader, no proprietary platforms. If you trade on anything else, this is a hard blocker.

It's worth noting there is no free trial or practice challenge available. You're paying real money from day one. For traders who want to test the waters before committing, this is a meaningful drawback — firms like Apex Trader Funding offer evaluation-free funded models that sidestep this issue entirely. You can see how the two stack up in the Apex Trader Funding vs Blue Guardian comparison.

What You Actually Pay: Fees and Hidden Costs

The prop firm industry has a reputation for layering on fees that aren't obvious at signup. Here's an honest accounting of what Blue Guardian charges and where traders sometimes get surprised:

Challenge Fee

The upfront challenge fee is your primary cost. This is non-refundable if you fail the evaluation. Some firms offer a refund on your first funded account payout — Blue Guardian's specific refund policy should be confirmed directly on their site at the time of purchase, as terms can update.

No Monthly Subscription on Funded Accounts

Once you pass the evaluation and receive a funded account, there are no ongoing monthly fees. Your only cost was the original challenge fee. This is a cleaner model than firms that charge recurring platform or data fees.

Profit Split Structure

The 80% split applies to net profits. Losses aren't charged back to you beyond the drawdown rules enforced during trading — if you blow a funded account by breaching the drawdown limit, you lose access to that account, but you're not billed for the loss. You would need to repurchase a challenge to try again.

Withdrawal Processing

Blue Guardian processes withdrawals periodically. Check their current payout schedule — some firms pay monthly, others bi-weekly. Delays between earning profits and receiving them aren't a "hidden fee" exactly, but they affect your cash flow planning.

For a deeper dive into the platform's full feature set and trading conditions, the Blue Guardian review covers the funded account rules in detail.

If you're weighing Blue Guardian against specific alternatives, the AquaFunded vs Blue Guardian comparison and the Blue Guardian vs BrightFunded comparison break down the differences side by side with current data.

Who Should Pay for Blue Guardian

Blue Guardian is not for everyone. Here's an honest breakdown of the trader profiles this firm serves well — and those who should look elsewhere.

Strong Fit: Algorithmic and EA Traders

If you run automated strategies, Blue Guardian's full permission for Expert Advisors during both phases is a significant advantage. Many firms allow EAs during evaluation but restrict them on funded accounts, or require specific EA approval. If your edge is automated, confirm the current EA rules directly with the firm before purchasing, but their stated policy is permissive.

Strong Fit: Experienced Risk Managers

The two-phase model rewards disciplined traders who can demonstrate consistency over two consecutive evaluation periods. If your trading record shows steady risk management rather than home-run swings, the evaluation structure aligns with how you already trade.

Strong Fit: Forex and Multi-Asset Traders

Blue Guardian's instrument coverage targets forex pairs and multi-asset traders. If your primary market is equities or futures with specific platform requirements, the MT4/MT5 limitation may be a deal-breaker.

Poor Fit: Beginners Needing a Practice Run

There's no free trial or practice challenge. If you're not yet confident in your consistency, you're paying to learn — and challenge fees add up quickly when you're in development mode as a trader.

Poor Fit: Non-MT4/MT5 Traders

This is a hard constraint. The platform limitation to MetaTrader 4 and MT5 only rules out traders who use cTrader, NinjaTrader, or proprietary platforms. There's no workaround here.

Blue Guardian Pros and Cons for 2026

After reviewing the pricing structure and trading conditions, here's the clear-eyed summary:

Pros

  • Transparent two-phase rules: The evaluation criteria are clearly documented with no moving goalposts. You know exactly what you need to hit in Phase 1 and Phase 2 before you start.
  • Competitive 80% profit split: Above the industry average for standard plans. Many comparable firms default to 70-75%.
  • Full EA and automated strategy permission: A genuine differentiator for algorithmic traders who face restrictions at other firms.
  • No ongoing funded account fees: Once you're funded, the challenge fee was your total upfront cost. No monthly platform fees eating into your profits.

Cons

  • No free trial or practice challenge: Every attempt costs real money. Repeated failures are expensive.
  • MT4 and MT5 only: Platform restriction limits accessibility for a segment of traders.
  • 3.8/5 rating: Solid but not market-leading. There are higher-rated firms available depending on your specific trading style and requirements.

Final Recommendation: Is Blue Guardian Worth It?

Blue Guardian earns its place in the funded trading market, but it's a targeted fit rather than a universal recommendation.

Pay for Blue Guardian if: You trade forex or multi-asset instruments on MT4 or MT5, you run or plan to run automated strategies, and you have a documented track record of disciplined risk management. The 80% profit split and transparent evaluation rules make it a genuinely competitive option for the right trader profile.

Look elsewhere if: You need a free trial to build confidence before paying, you trade on platforms other than MetaTrader, or you're earlier in your trading development and expect to need multiple evaluation attempts. The cost of repeated failed challenges without a practice option can significantly inflate your total spend before seeing a funded account.

For a complete picture of Blue Guardian's trading conditions, payout history, and user experience, read the full Blue Guardian review before making your final decision. The pricing structure is only one part of the evaluation — rule consistency, customer support, and payout reliability matter just as much when you're trusting a firm with your trading capital.

If you're still comparing options, shortlisting Blue Guardian alongside two or three alternatives and running the numbers on realistic profit scenarios given your account size and expected monthly returns will give you a clearer picture than any single review can provide.
